turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Gorsuch has the opinion this morning in case 21-1168 Mallory v. Norfolk Southern Railway.

turns.name: JUSTICE GORSUCH
turns.text: Robert Mallory worked for Norfolk Southern Railway Company for almost 20 years, first in Ohio and later in Virginia. He believes his work exposed into harmful carcinogens and caused him to contract cancer. Seeking relief for these injuries, he sued his former employer. But Mr. Mallory did not sue in Ohio or Virginia instead he sued in Pennsylvania. Pennsylvania Law expressly permits suits like Mr. Mallory's and provides that an out-of-state corporation wishing to do extensive business in the Commonwealth must register with state authorities. In doing so, a company must agree to appear in Pennsylvania Courts on any cause of action brought against it there, no matter who's bringing suit or where the underlying cause of action arose. Consistent with the statutory regime, Norfolk Southern registered in 1998 appointed a local agent to receive service of process and agreed that it would ?be deemed located in Pennsylvania for purposes of any suit?. When it came time to respond to Mr. Mallory's claim however, the company argued none of that mattered because Mr. Mallory's lawsuit concerned events in Ohio and Virginia and because both Mr. Mallory and Norfolk Southern were residents of Virginia, the company said any attempt by a Pennsylvania Court to exercise personal jurisdiction over it would violate the due process clause of the Fourteenth Amendment. But here's the problem. Over a hundred years ago, this Court rejected a similar argument by a corporate defendant in a case called Pennsylvania Fire. There a mining company from Arizona and an insurance company from Pennsylvania entered into a contract in Colorado. When a dispute arose about the contract, the mining company sued the insurer but not in Colorado, Arizona or Pennsylvania. It sued in Missouri. That state like Pennsylvania here required out-of-state companies to appoint in-state agents to accept service of process in any suit brought against them and the insurance company had done just that. When the insurance company nevertheless objected to suit on due process grounds, this Court unanimously entered opinion by Justice Holmes rejected its appeal because the company had agreed to accept service in Missouri, the Court held the due process clause did not stand in the way. Our case is on all fours with Pennsylvania Fire and that precedent controls the outcome of this case. Recognizing as much, Norfolk Southern candidly asks us to overrule Pennsylvania Fire. To smooth away, the company argues that Pennsylvania Fire's Foundations were undermined by this Court's later decision in International Shoe. We disagree. In fact, International Shoe merely set out a framework for plaintiffs to obtain personal jurisdiction over corporate defendants that have not consented to jurisdiction in a forum state. International Shoe and its progeny do not speak to and hardly prohibit consent-based statutes like the one at issue here and in Pennsylvania Fire. Notably to International Shoe and are later decisions applying its framework have emphasized that the due process clause isn't measured by one particular formal test or another but by traditional conceptions of fair play and substantial justice. And here a Pennsylvania Court's exercise of jurisdiction is hardly inappropriate when measured against that standard. At the time Mr. Mallory brought his action, Norfolk Southern employed nearly 5,000 people in Pennsylvania. It maintained more than 2,400 miles of track and it operated North America's largest locomotive repair shop there. The company even proclaimed itself a proud part of ?The Pennsylvania community?. Given all this, the company cannot now fairly pretend to be a stranger to that state. For these reasons and others detailed in our opinion today, we reject Norfolk Southern's due process claim of immunity from suit. The Judgment of the Pennsylvania Supreme Court is vacated and the case is remanded. Justice Jackson has written a concurring opinion. Justice Alito has written an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment. Justice Barrett has altered a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice, Justice Kagan and Justice Kavanaugh joined.
